peer_exemplars.reasoning: High conscientiousness and authoritarian submission support structured oversight, but very low openness and low digital literacy temper enthusiasm for complex transparency mandates.

High extraversion and low agreeableness (HEXACO) combined with strong anti-elitism and distrust of institutions (trust in science at 2.57) produce moderate support for transparency as a check on opaque power, but low trust in government (7.77) tempers enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

Very low openness, high authoritarian submission, and strong system justification drive skepticism toward forcing platform transparency, as it challenges existing power structures and feels like unnecessary interference.

High openness and very low agreeableness, combined with strong libertarian anti-censorship orientation and deep distrust of institutions, produce mild support for transparency as a check on power, but skepticism that any mandate would be implemented fairly tempers enthusiasm.
High openness and low agreeableness drive a cautious but clear support for transparency, tempered by moderate trust in institutions and a libertarian skepticism of state control.

High libertarian anti-censorship orientation (98.33) and strong privacy/anti-surveillance stance (82.47) support transparency, but very low trust in government (16.53) and institutions tempers enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

Very low openness and high conscientiousness plus a dangerous worldview motivate cautious support for transparency as a system-stabilizing reform, but low agreeableness and strong anti-egalitarianism keep enthusiasm from being strong.

High openness and low conscientiousness, combined with strong anti-elitism and a desire for system disruption, drive moderate support for algorithmic transparency as a check on opaque corporate power.

High dutifulness and system justification combine with strong competitive jungle worldview to back clear rules against deceptive actors, while low libertarianism and moderate civic nationalism temper any maximalism.

Strong authoritarian law-and-order preference and high threat sensitivity make this person clearly favor disclosure to police inauthentic behavior, though low trust in institutions tempers maximal enthusiasm.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high agreeableness and tender-mindedness drive support for educational interventions, while high dutifulness and conventionalism align with promoting responsible citizenship.

High conscientiousness (70.56) and moderate openness (57.59) combined with strong care/harm moral foundation (93.03) and a competitive worldview (87.64) produce strong support for equipping citizens to navigate information, seen as a protective measure.
Very low openness and low digital literacy make this seem irrelevant or burdensome, though a slight pull from equity values prevents strong opposition.

High openness, advanced digital literacy, and strong value on personal growth align with empowering individuals, while deep distrust of institutions makes a top-down education program less appealing but still clearly supported as a bottom-up solution.
Very high openness and advanced digital literacy, paired with a strong sense of duty and community engagement, drive robust support for educational interventions.

High openness to experience (59.26) and strong care/harm compassion (86.84) align with educational interventions, and active civic engagement (civic_engagement: Active) reinforces support for empowering citizens.

Very low openness and a tradition-oriented socio-cultural tilt make educational interventions seem naive and likely to challenge established values, despite high conscientiousness and moderate trust in institutions.
High openness, strong altruism, and a philanthropic HEXAD profile drive clear support for media literacy as a prosocial, empowering solution, despite low digital literacy and intermittent access.

Skepticism of government efficacy (trust <5%) and populist anti-elitism create mild opposition to government-funded tools, though the open-source nature aligns with libertarian values and slightly moderates resistance.

High disruptor traits and anti-censorship orientation support user choice, but low openness and high authoritarian submission limit enthusiasm for mandated changes.

Fairness/equality values and a dangerous worldview support labeling to prevent deception, but low trust in government and anti-censorship leanings keep support moderate.

Competing forces: high openness to new experiences and concern for deceptive manipulation pull somewhat positive, while libertarian anti-censorship and deep distrust of mandate enforcers pull negative, resulting in near-neutral ambivalence.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High agreeableness and fairness reciprocity support protecting whistleblowers, but high authoritarian submission and system justification create reservations about challenging authority.

High disruptor traits and fairness values weakly support protections, but low trust in institutions and high authoritarian submission create ambivalence.

Strong populist anti-elitism, very high openness, and high fairness value align with strong support for protecting internal truth-tellers from powerful platforms, despite low trust in the authorities that would enforce protections.

High dutifulness and system justification back protections as procedural fairness, but low openness, competitive worldview, and collective narcissism dampen enthusiasm, resulting in mild support.
Strong support is driven by high openness, a disruptor's desire to expose hidden truths, strong altruism, and a populist anti-elite stance, despite low institutional trust.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Extremely high system justification and trust in government, combined with low populist attitudes, produce strong opposition to distrusting elections.

Extremely low trust in government (7.77) and judiciary (6.29) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77), a competitive worldview (87.64), and strong ethnic nationalism (92.61) produce very strong distrust of electoral processes, viewing them as rigged by elites.
Very low trust in government, high authoritarian aggression, and a dangerous worldview fuel clear distrust in electoral integrity, seeing it as compromised by elites.

Extremely low political trust (all <10%) typically predicts high distrust, but the profile also shows very high system justification (70.21%) and very low dangerous-worldview threat sensitivity (5.11%), together with authoritarian submission (88.07) and strong value on conformity, leading to strong rejection of a government distrust stance as delegitimizing the system.
Moderate trust in government and political system, combined with low populist attitudes, produce mild disagreement with election distrust, though some skepticism remains.

Extremely low trust in government (16.53), judiciary (8.26), and police (13.22) combined with very low political efficacy (3.69) and high collective narcissism (92.44) produce near-maximal distrust in electoral integrity.

Moderate trust in government and political system combined with high system justification and moderate external efficacy buffer against election distrust, but low openness and collective narcissism allow some skepticism to persist.
Strong distrust is consistent with very low political trust, high anti-elitism, low system justification, and a competitive-jungle worldview that sees elections as rigged by elites.
